danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy units appear on screen within visual radius of friendly units; disappear from view when outside radius
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate combat threat; proximity to enemy forces

danger_signals.visual_form: Black/unexplored territory appears completely obscured on map
danger_signals.meaning: Unknown danger; potential enemy presence in unscouted areas

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual degradation of building sprites when damaged (last-seen state)
danger_signals.meaning: Ongoing attack or loss of structural integrity; strategic vulnerability

danger_signals.visual_form: Gold mines and forests visually diminish or disappear as harvested
danger_signals.meaning: Economic pressure; limited time before economic collapse

danger_signals.visual_form: UI feedback when insufficient farms/food available for unit production
danger_signals.meaning: Economic constraint; inability to expand military

safety_signals.visual_form: Previously explored areas remain visible in gray tones even without current unit presence
safety_signals.meaning: Known safe passage; mapped territory free from fog of war

safety_signals.visual_form: Workers harvesting resources visibly moving between resource nodes and halls
Structures fully rendered and operational on screen
Faction-colored structures visible on map and minimap

faction_indicators.color_identity: Blue/human faction colors (presumed from RTS convention)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Town Hall as primary economic structure; human-styled architecture
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Orcs appear as distinctly alien/green-skinned invaders with crude structures
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Medieval human settlements; human-proportioned buildings; organized town layouts

faction_indicators.color_identity: Red/orc faction colors (presumed from RTS convention)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Great Hall as primary economic structure; tribal/organic architecture
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Humans appear as organized defenders with structured fortifications
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Tribal encampments; crude/organic building designs; less refined settlements

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Workers actively harvesting; basic structures (Town Hall/Great Hall, Barracks) visible; minimal unit presence on map
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Slow unit production; focus on resource gathering; limited military activity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Advanced buildings constructed (shipyards, tech buildings); diverse unit types present; expanded territory control
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Increased unit production rates; unlocked advanced unit types; faction-specific strategic advantages emerge (Orc ground superiority, Human naval/spellcaster advantage)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Maximum unit diversity visible; heavy building damage; contested territory; naval units on shorelines
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Continuous combat; resource scarcity visible; advanced spell usage; strategic unit positioning

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Orc units deep in Human territory; Human defensive structures (farms used as walls) prominent; mixed faction buildings visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Active enemy unit presence in explored areas; defensive positioning; battle formations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Empty resource nodes; no visible worker activity; stalled building construction
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: No new unit production possible; infrastructure deterioration visible; military inactivity

environmental_storytelling.example: Gold mines and forests become visually depleted or removed from map, showing battle impact on land and economic desperation
Buildings display last-seen state (damaged/undamaged) even when unobserved, creating narrative of ongoing unseen conflict
Human organized settlements vs. Orc tribal encampments communicate the civilized vs. invasion narrative through building style and layout
Minimap and main view show faction-colored structures indicating who controls which regions; no-man's-lands appear as contested space
Black unexplored areas represent fear of the unknown; grayed-out explored areas represent hope of controlled space

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Visible continuous movement between resource nodes and halls; indicates economic stability and opponent capability
Visible on minimap; represents imminent threat; triggers defensive response
Indicates tech progression reached; reveals faction's current military strength tier
Partial sprite rendering; indicates future capability unlock; communicates strategic planning
Visual spell effects; indicates advanced tech reached; asymmetric advantage for Humans in magic capability

notes: Warcraft II uses classic RTS visibility mechanics (fog of war, unit visual radius) as core world-state communication systems. Faction differentiation is primarily architectural and structural rather than color-coded in early RTS tradition. The game communicates danger through presence/absence of units in explored space and safety through resource availability and building integrity. Political situation (invasion narrative) is communicated through faction presence in territory and building type distribution across the map. The persistent building last-seen-state mechanic creates narrative tension about unseen enemy activity.
